full_response:
We start from the Schwarzschild line‐element (signature −+++)

ds² = −(1 − 2GM/r) dt² + (1 − 2GM/r)⁻¹dr² + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).

To decide which coordinate is timelike we look at the signs of g_tt and g_rr.

1.  Region I (usual exterior, r > 2GM):  
   1 − 2GM/r > 0 ⇒ g_tt < 0, g_rr > 0  
   → t timelike, r spacelike.

2.  Region II (interior, 0 < r < 2GM):  
   1 − 2GM/r < 0 ⇒ g_tt > 0, g_rr < 0  
   → t spacelike, r timelike.

Now extend the coordinate r through the true curvature singularity r = 0 into the mathematically defined domain r < 0.  
For r < 0 (with the physical mass M > 0 kept positive)

2GM/r < 0  (because r is negative)  
⇒ 1 − 2GM/r = 1 + |2GM/r|  > 1  > 0.

Hence in the whole range r < 0 we again have

g_tt = −(1 − 2GM/r) < 0, g_rr = (1 − 2GM/r)⁻¹ > 0 .

Therefore t is once more timelike and r is spacelike; the metric never swaps their roles in this “fifth region”.

Consequently, although one may formally write the metric for r < 0, there is no part of that region in which r becomes the timelike coordinate.

\boxed{\text{For r<0 (with positive mass) r always remains spacelike and t timelike; there is no region where r is timelike.}}

Confidence: 71%
extracted_answer: \text{For r<0 (with positive mass) r always remains spacelike and t timelike; there is no region where r is timelike.}
